title: Setup Python Environment and Dependencies
description: Install required packages for e-graph construction, treewidth computation, and extraction algorithms. Use CPU-only packages to stay within resource constraints.

steps.step2: Install core dependencies: pip install networkx numpy scipy pandas matplotlib
steps.step3: Install ILP solver: pip install pulp (for optimal extraction baseline)
steps.step4: Install treewidth library: pip install networkx[all] (for min-fill heuristics)
steps.step5: Verify installations: python -c 'import networkx; import pulp; import numpy; print("OK")'

title: Download and Prepare Polybench/C Benchmarks
description: Download Polybench/C 4.2.1 benchmark suite and extract kernel information for e-graph construction. Focus on linear algebra and stencil kernels where memory bandwidth matters.

steps.step2: Extract: tar -xzf polybench.tar.gz && mv polybench-c-4.2.1 polybench
steps.step3: Select 10 kernels: gemm, 2mm, 3mm, gemver, gesummv, mvt, syrk, syr2k, jacobi-2d, fdtd-2d
steps.step4: Extract kernel metadata: python scripts/extract_kernels.py --kernels gemm,2mm,3mm,gemver,gesummv,mvt,syrk,syr2k,jacobi-2d,fdtd-2d --output data/kernels.json
steps.step5: Parse loop structures and array accesses from kernel sources
steps.step6: Generate kernel statistics: array dimensions, loop nests, memory access patterns

title: Build Hierarchical E-Graph Representation
description: Construct hierarchical e-graphs for each kernel at three levels: loop nest, function, and module. Apply computational and layout rewrite rules.

steps.step1: Implement core e-graph data structures: ENode, EClass, EGraph classes
steps.step2: Implement computational rewrite rules: algebraic simplification, loop interchange, distribution
steps.step3: Implement layout transformation rules: AoS↔SoA conversion, tiling, padding with correctness verification
steps.step4: Build Level-1 (Loop Nest) e-graphs: saturate each loop nest independently
steps.step5: Build Level-2 (Function) e-graphs: combine loop nests with function-level alternatives
steps.step6: Verify soundness: check that all rewrite rules preserve semantics via structural equivalence
steps.step7: Export e-graphs to JSON format for analysis

title: Measure Treewidth of Hierarchical E-Graphs
description: Test Hypothesis H1: Real-world program e-graphs exhibit low treewidth (≤10) when rewrite rules are applied hierarchically. Compute treewidth using min-fill-in heuristic.

steps.step1: Convert each e-graph to conflict graph: vertices=e-classes, edges=data dependencies
steps.step3: Record treewidth, number of bags, and tree decomposition structure
steps.step4: Measure e-graph size: number of e-classes, e-nodes, edges
steps.step5: Compare flat vs. hierarchical: measure treewidth reduction from hierarchical decomposition
steps.step6: Run with 3 random seeds for rewrite rule application order (seeds: 42, 123, 456)
steps.step7: Compute statistics: mean, std treewidth per kernel; percentage with treewidth ≤10

success_criteria: At least 70% of benchmark e-graphs have treewidth ≤ 10 under hierarchical decomposition

title: Baseline 1: Greedy Extraction
description: Implement and run greedy extraction as simple baseline. Selects locally optimal e-node at each e-class without global optimization.

steps.step1: Implement greedy extraction: for each e-class, pick e-node with minimum local cost
steps.step2: Define instruction-count cost model: add=1, mul=1, load=1, store=1, div=3
steps.step3: Run greedy extraction on all 10 kernels at both hierarchy levels
steps.step4: Record extraction time and total cost for each kernel
steps.step5: Run with 3 random seeds (42, 123, 456) for rule application order
steps.step6: Compute mean and std for extraction time and solution cost

title: Baseline 2: ILP-Based Extraction (Optimal)
description: Implement ILP formulation for optimal extraction using PuLP. This provides the gold standard for solution quality comparison.

steps.step1: Implement ILP formulation: binary variable per e-node, constraints for single selection per e-class
steps.step2: Add parent-child constraints: if e-node selected, exactly one child from each child e-class
steps.step3: Set timeout to 60 seconds per extraction (limit for feasibility)
steps.step4: Run ILP on all kernels with instruction-count cost model
steps.step5: Record: solution cost, solve time, solver status (optimal/timeout/infeasible)
steps.step6: For timeouts, record best solution found within time limit

notes: ILP may timeout on larger e-graphs; use best-effort solution when timeout occurs

title: Baseline 3: Sequential Optimization (Compute then Layout)
description: Implement sequential optimization as strong baseline: first optimize computation, then apply data layout transformations separately.

steps.step1: Phase 1: Apply computational rewrites, extract with instruction-count cost model
steps.step2: Phase 2: Apply layout transformations to extracted program, optimize memory cost
steps.step3: Record total cost (compute + layout) for each kernel
steps.step4: Compare against joint optimization to measure benefit of unified approach

title: Treewidth-Aware Extraction Algorithm
description: Test Hypothesis H3: Treewidth-aware extraction provides compile times competitive with greedy while achieving solution quality within 10% of optimal ILP.

steps.step1: Implement treewidth-aware extraction: dynamic programming on tree decomposition
steps.step2: For each bag in tree decomposition, enumerate valid selections (e-node per e-class)
steps.step3: Apply DP: for each bag, compute optimal cost given parent bag selection
steps.step5: Handle e-classes appearing in multiple bags via consistency constraints
steps.step6: Run on all kernels with instruction-count cost model
steps.step7: Record: extraction time, solution cost, treewidth used

fallback: If DP implementation too complex, use beam search (width=10) as fallback

title: Joint Compute and Layout Optimization
description: Test Hypothesis H2: Joint optimization of computation and data layout achieves better memory bandwidth utilization than sequential optimization.

steps.step1: Implement memory-aware cost model: Cost = compute_cost + memory_penalty
steps.step2: Memory cost components: spatial locality, temporal locality, stride pattern
steps.step3: Layout penalties: AoS=1.2, SoA=1.0, tiled=0.9 (normalized to cache behavior)
steps.step4: Apply joint optimization: use layout-aware rewrites during saturation, joint cost during extraction
steps.step5: Run joint optimization with treewidth-aware extraction on all kernels
steps.step6: Simulate memory bandwidth: estimate bytes accessed per useful computation
steps.step7: Record: total cost, memory cost component, compute cost component

success_criteria: Joint optimization achieves ≥10% lower total cost than sequential on memory-bound kernels

title: Ablation 1: Hierarchy Levels
description: Measure impact of hierarchical decomposition by comparing flat vs. 2-level hierarchy.

steps.step1: Flat e-graph: build monolithic e-graph for entire function
steps.step2: 2-level hierarchy: separate loop nest and function levels
steps.step3: Compare: treewidth, extraction time, solution quality
steps.step4: Run both configurations on all kernels
steps.step5: Report treewidth reduction from hierarchical decomposition

title: Ablation 2: Extraction Algorithms Comparison
description: Compare all extraction algorithms: Greedy, Beam Search (fallback), Treewidth-Aware, and ILP.

steps.step1: Run all 4 extraction methods on same e-graphs
steps.step2: Metrics: extraction time (ms), solution cost, quality ratio vs ILP
steps.step3: Quality ratio = (algorithm_cost - ilp_cost) / ilp_cost (lower is better)
steps.step4: Time ratio = algorithm_time / greedy_time (lower is better)
steps.step5: Generate Pareto frontier: time vs quality tradeoff
steps.step6: Identify which algorithm provides best time/quality balance

title: Ablation 3: Layout Transformation Rules
description: Measure impact of layout transformations by comparing with/without layout rules.

steps.step1: Without layout: apply only computational rewrite rules
steps.step2: With layout: apply both computational and layout transformation rules
steps.step3: Use memory-aware cost model for both configurations
steps.step4: Compare: total cost, memory cost component, solution diversity
steps.step5: Count: number of layout e-nodes created, unique layout alternatives explored

title: Ablation 4: Cost Models
description: Compare different cost models: instruction count vs. memory-aware vs. combined.

steps.step1: Instruction-count only: count arithmetic and memory instructions
steps.step2: Memory-aware only: focus on cache behavior and bandwidth
steps.step3: Combined: weighted sum with tunable parameter α
steps.step4: Vary α from 0 (pure compute) to 1 (pure memory) in 0.25 increments
steps.step5: Measure how different costs correlate with simulated performance
steps.step6: Identify optimal α for memory-bound vs compute-bound kernels

title: Sensitivity Analysis: Rewrite Rule Application Order
description: Test robustness to rewrite rule application order using multiple random seeds.

steps.step1: Run full pipeline with 5 random seeds: 42, 123, 456, 789, 101112
steps.step2: Measure variance in: e-graph size, treewidth, extraction time, solution cost
steps.step3: Report coefficient of variation (CV = std/mean) for each metric
steps.step4: High CV indicates sensitivity to rule order; low CV indicates robustness

title: Statistical Analysis and Hypothesis Testing
description: Perform statistical tests to validate hypotheses with appropriate significance levels.

steps.step1: Load all experimental results from previous steps
steps.step2: Compute mean, std, 95% confidence intervals for all metrics
steps.step3: H1 test: Binomial test for proportion of kernels with treewidth ≤ 10
steps.step4: H2 test: Paired t-test comparing joint vs sequential optimization costs
steps.step5: H3 test: One-sample t-test comparing solution quality ratio to 0.10 threshold
steps.step6: Effect size: compute Cohen's d for practical significance
steps.step7: Generate summary table with all statistical results

title: Success Criteria Validation
description: Validate all success criteria defined in the proposal and determine hypothesis confirmation/refutation.

steps.step1: H1 criterion: Check if ≥70% kernels have treewidth ≤ 10
steps.step2: H2 criterion: Check if joint optimization achieves ≥10% improvement over sequential
steps.step3: H3 criterion: Check if extraction is ≤5× slower than greedy with ≥90% ILP quality
steps.step4: Refutation check: Verify treewidth not >15, joint not worse than sequential
steps.step5: Document any limitations or confounding factors

title: Generate Paper Figures
description: Create publication-quality figures for the research paper.

steps.step1: Figure 1: Treewidth distribution across kernels (histogram + boxplot)
steps.step2: Figure 2: Treewidth comparison - flat vs hierarchical (bar chart)
steps.step3: Figure 3: Extraction time vs solution quality scatter plot (all methods)
steps.step4: Figure 4: Speedup over baselines (LLVM-style bar chart with error bars)
steps.step5: Figure 5: Ablation study results (grouped bar chart)
steps.step6: Figure 6: Cost model comparison - compute vs memory cost breakdown
steps.step7: Table 1: Main results table with all metrics (LaTeX format)
steps.step8: Table 2: Statistical significance table (p-values, confidence intervals)

title: Generate Summary Report
description: Create comprehensive markdown report summarizing all experiments and findings.

steps.step1: Summarize experimental setup: benchmarks, baselines, metrics
steps.step2: Report H1 results: treewidth measurements with statistics
steps.step3: Report H2 results: joint vs sequential optimization comparison
steps.step4: Report H3 results: extraction time/quality tradeoff analysis
